questions.question: Explain the purpose of a destructor in C++. When is it invoked and what typical tasks does it perform? Provide a brief example.
keyPoints: Destructor cleans up resources (memory, files) when object goes out of scope
Automatically called; cannot be overloaded; has no parameters or return type
Execution order: reverse of construction (member destructors before base destructors)

questions.question: What is an initializer list in a constructor? Why is it preferred over assignment inside the constructor body? Give an example.
keyPoints: Initializer list directly initializes members before the constructor body runs
Required for const and reference members, and for efficiency (avoids extra default construction)
Example: class A { const int x; A(int v) : x(v) {} };

questions.question: Discuss the order of constructor and destructor calls in a multi-level inheritance hierarchy. Include the role of virtual destructors when a base class pointer points to a derived object. Provide a code example illustrating this order.
keyPoints: Constructors run from most‑base to most‑derived; destructors run reverse (derived to base)
If base class destructor is not virtual, deleting a derived object through base pointer causes undefined behaviour
Virtual destructor ensures proper derived destructor is called, then base destructor
Example: class Base{virtual ~Base(){}}; class Derived: public Base{ ~Derived(){}}; Base* p = new Derived; delete p; // calls ~Derived then ~Base

questions.question: Explain the copy assignment operator in C++. How does it differ from the copy constructor? When is it invoked? Also discuss why a base class should have a virtual destructor when used polymorphically, with reference to assignment and destruction.
keyPoints: Copy assignment operator is used when an existing object is assigned a new value of the same type; signature: Class& operator=(const Class&)
Differs from copy constructor (initializes new object) – assignment operates on already constructed object
Invoked in statements like a = b; or when passing/returning by value (if copy elision not applied)
Virtual destructor needed so that delete on base pointer calls derived destructor, ensuring resources (including any owned dynamic memory) are correctly released
